section.title: Stages of Israel's Journey
section.sectionRationale: Before Israel crosses into the land, Moses records, at the LORD's command, the complete list of every stage of the journey from Egypt to the plains of Moab. This travel record closes with a direct command to drive out Canaan's inhabitants and destroy their idols completely, warning that anything left behind will become a lasting snare.
section.thesis: Moses records every stage of Israel's forty-year journey from Rameses to the plains of Moab, a written testimony to a road fully walked under God's leading, and the LORD follows the record with a direct command to take the land, destroy every trace of Canaanite idolatry, and divide the inheritance by lot, warning that anything left standing will become a thorn and a snare.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the journey from Egypt to the Wilderness of Sin; the long list of stations through the wilderness years; the journey from Mount Hor, including Aaron's death, to the plains of Moab; and the closing command to take the land and destroy every trace of idolatry.

verses.text: These are the {{journeys|journeys}} of the Israelites when they came out of the land of Egypt by their divisions under the leadership of Moses and Aaron.
At the LORD's command, Moses recorded the stages of their journey. These are the stages listed by their starting points:

units.label: From Egypt to the Wilderness of Sin

verses.text: On the fifteenth day of the first month, on the day after the Passover, the Israelites set out from Rameses. They marched out defiantly in full view of all the Egyptians,
who were burying all their firstborn, whom the LORD had struck down among them; for the LORD had executed judgment against their gods.
The Israelites set out from Rameses and camped at Succoth.
They set out from Succoth and camped at Etham, on the edge of the wilderness.
They set out from Etham and turned back to Pi-hahiroth, opposite Baal-zephon, and they camped near Migdol.
They set out from Pi-hahiroth and crossed through the sea, into the wilderness, and they journeyed three days into the Wilderness of Etham and camped at Marah.
They set out from Marah and came to Elim, where there were twelve springs and seventy palm trees, and they camped there.
They set out from Elim and camped by the Red Sea.
They set out from the Red Sea and camped in the Desert of Sin.

units.label: The long wilderness years
units.note: camp after camp for forty years

media.alt: A faint dotted line of footprints winding across a desert map toward a distant river
media.caption: Every camp along the way, written down at the LORD's own command.

verses.text: They set out from the Desert of Sin and camped at Dophkah.
They set out from Dophkah and camped at Alush.
They set out from Alush and camped at Rephidim, where there was no water for the people to drink.
They set out from Rephidim and camped in the Wilderness of Sinai.
They set out from the Wilderness of Sinai and camped at Kibroth-hattaavah.
They set out from Kibroth-hattaavah and camped at Hazeroth.
They set out from Hazeroth and camped at Rithmah.
They set out from Rithmah and camped at Rimmon-perez.
They set out from Rimmon-perez and camped at Libnah.
They set out from Libnah and camped at Rissah.
They set out from Rissah and camped at Kehelathah.
They set out from Kehelathah and camped at Mount Shepher.
They set out from Mount Shepher and camped at Haradah.
They set out from Haradah and camped at Makheloth.
They set out from Makheloth and camped at Tahath.
They set out from Tahath and camped at Terah.
They set out from Terah and camped at Mithkah.
They set out from Mithkah and camped at Hashmonah.
They set out from Hashmonah and camped at Moseroth.
They set out from Moseroth and camped at Bene-jaakan.
They set out from Bene-jaakan and camped at Hor-haggidgad.
They set out from Hor-haggidgad and camped at Jotbathah.
They set out from Jotbathah and camped at Abronah.
They set out from Abronah and camped at Ezion-geber.
They set out from Ezion-geber and camped at Kadesh in the Wilderness of Zin.

units.label: Mount Hor to the plains of Moab

verses.text: They set out from Kadesh and camped at Mount Hor, on the outskirts of the land of Edom.
At the LORD's command, Aaron the priest climbed Mount Hor and died there on the first day of the fifth month, in the fortieth year after the Israelites had come out of the land of Egypt.
Aaron was 123 years old when he died on Mount Hor.
Now the Canaanite king of Arad, who lived in the Negev in the land of Canaan, heard that the Israelites were coming.
And the Israelites set out from Mount Hor and camped at Zalmonah.
They set out from Zalmonah and camped at Punon.
They set out from Punon and camped at Oboth.
They set out from Oboth and camped at Iye-abarim on the border of Moab.
They set out from Iyim and camped at Dibon-gad.
They set out from Dibon-gad and camped at Almon-diblathaim.
They set out from Almon-diblathaim and camped in the mountains of Abarim facing Nebo.
They set out from the mountains of Abarim and camped on the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho.
And there on the plains of Moab they camped by the Jordan, from Beth-jeshimoth to Abel-shittim.

units.label: The command to take the land

verses.text: On the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho, the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and tell them: When you cross the Jordan into the land of Canaan,
you must drive out before you all the inhabitants of the land, destroy all their carved images and cast idols, and demolish all their {{highplaces|high places}}.
You are to take possession of the land and settle in it, for I have given you the land to possess.

And you are to divide the land by lot according to your clans. Give a larger inheritance to a larger clan and a smaller inheritance to a smaller one. Whatever falls to each one by lot will be his. You will receive an inheritance according to the tribes of your fathers.

But if you do not drive out the inhabitants of the land before you, those you allow to remain will become barbs in your eyes and thorns in your sides; they will harass you in the land where you settle.
And then I will do to you what I had planned to do to them.”

journeys.gloss: Journeys, stages, settings-out. The chapter title itself in Hebrew is these masa, marking each new departure rather than each destination, emphasizing that Israel's whole wilderness experience was a series of faithful movements forward under God's own command, not aimless wandering.

highplaces.gloss: High places, elevated sites used for pagan worship. God commands their complete demolition, not merely their disuse, since these sites had already been consecrated to false gods and, if left standing, would become an ongoing temptation to Israel's own worship.

basic.context: Standing on the edge of Canaan, Moses writes down the complete record of every place Israel camped across forty years, from the night they left Egypt to their current camp on the plains of Moab. Immediately after this record, God commands Israel to take the land decisively, destroying every trace of Canaanite idolatry rather than leaving it to become a future snare.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, deliberate historical record, commanded by God Himself to be written down, not merely narrated. Its plain, repetitive style, they set out from this place and camped at that one, underscores that every stage of the journey mattered enough to God to be permanently recorded, even the places modern readers cannot always locate.
basic.originalLanguages: The list is framed as masa, journeys or settings-out, naming each new departure rather than lingering on each destination, a subtle emphasis that Israel's story is one of continual forward movement under God's leading. The closing command to demolish every bamot, high place, insists on complete removal of pagan worship sites, not mere neglect.
basic.typology: That God commanded this journey to be written down for the record anticipates the New Testament's own use of Israel's wilderness history as a permanent, instructive testimony for later generations of God's people, written down for our instruction, 1 Corinthians 10:11.
basic.christ: While this chapter contains no direct messianic prophecy, its record of a long, faithful journey completed under God's leading anticipates the pattern of the whole Christian life as a pilgrimage, a journey recorded and remembered, toward a promised inheritance, ultimately secured and guaranteed by Christ, Hebrews 11:13-16, 1 Peter 1:3-5.
basic.god: God cares enough about His people's entire journey, every single stop, to have it permanently recorded, showing that no stage of the wilderness years was wasted or forgotten by Him. His command to remove Canaan's idolatry completely also shows His unwavering concern that nothing be left standing that could later draw His people's hearts away from Him.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 10:1-11 uses Israel's entire wilderness journey, camp by camp, as a permanent instructive example for later believers. Hebrews 11:13-16 describes the life of faith as a journey toward a better, heavenly homeland, echoing the pattern of Israel's own long trek. Judges 2:1-3 records the consequence of Israel's failure to complete the warning given in verses 55-56.
basic.oneStory: Every stage of Israel's long wilderness journey mattered enough to God to be written down permanently, and their entry into the land depended on completing the task fully rather than leaving remnants of idolatry behind. This pattern, a recorded pilgrimage toward a promised inheritance, requiring full faithfulness along the way, runs through the whole Bible toward the better, heavenly homeland believers now journey toward in Christ.

context.literary: This chapter functions as a deliberate historical seam, summarizing the entire preceding narrative of Numbers as a single itinerary before turning, in its final verses, from retrospective record to forward-looking command about the conquest still ahead.
context.historical: Many of the roughly forty stations named here cannot be identified with certainty by modern geography, though several, such as Kadesh, Mount Hor, and the plains of Moab, correspond to locations well attested elsewhere in the biblical narrative and, in some cases, in the wider historical and archaeological record.
context.canonical: This itinerary becomes the basis for Israel's own historical memory of its wilderness formation, and its closing warning about incomplete conquest is fulfilled, tragically, in the book of Judges, where the very thorns and snares warned of here trouble Israel for generations.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide a permanent, God-commanded written record of Israel's entire wilderness journey, and to issue, alongside it, a clear and urgent command that entry into the land requires the complete removal of Canaanite idolatry, not partial or negotiated coexistence.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The itinerary itself is historical record, not moral instruction; the closing command regarding conquest and the destruction of idols is prescriptive specifically for Israel's unique conquest of Canaan under the old covenant, not a template for how believers relate to other cultures or peoples today.

debates.question: Why does God insist so strongly on the complete destruction of Canaanite religious sites rather than mere separation from them?

views.label: Idolatry's ongoing seductive danger required total removal, not mere avoidance
views.case: The warning that surviving inhabitants and their high places would become barbs and thorns, verse 55, and the earlier disaster at Peor, Numbers 25, already demonstrated how quickly proximity to Canaanite worship led Israel itself into compromise, making partial measures inadequate to the real danger.

views.label: This command belongs to Israel's unique, bounded conquest-era mission, not a universal principle of cultural engagement
views.case: This specific instruction to destroy religious sites is tied to Israel's unique covenant calling to possess this particular land as God's judgment on entrenched idolatry, Genesis 15:16, and does not translate into a template for how God's people relate to surrounding cultures in every era.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the command reflects the real spiritual danger idolatry posed to Israel's covenant faithfulness, a danger the following book of Judges shows was tragically realized when Israel failed to complete this task, without treating the specific command as a universal pattern for believers today, who advance God's kingdom through the gospel rather than conquest.

study.originalLanguages: The list is titled by masa, journeys or settings-out, a term emphasizing departure and forward movement rather than mere arrival, subtly framing the whole wilderness period as continuous progress under God's command rather than static wandering. The command to destroy every bamot, high place, demands total removal of these consecrated pagan sites, not simply Israel's own future avoidance of them.

typology.type: A journey fully recorded at God's own command
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates 1 Corinthians 10:1-11, where Israel's wilderness history is written down for the instruction of later believers facing similar tests.

typology.type: A long pilgrimage toward a promised inheritance
typology.fulfillment: Echoed in Hebrews 11:13-16, which describes the life of faith as a journey toward a better, heavenly homeland God has prepared.

study.christ: This chapter contains no direct prophecy of Christ, but its pattern, a long, fully recorded journey completed under God's faithful leading toward a promised inheritance, anticipates the shape of the whole Christian life. Hebrews 11:13-16 describes believers as strangers and exiles on the earth, seeking a better, heavenly country, a pilgrimage secured and guaranteed by Christ, who has gone ahead to prepare a place, John 14:2-3, just as Israel's own long journey led at last to the very edge of its promised land.
study.god: God values every stage of His people's journey enough to have it permanently recorded, showing that no season of wilderness testing was wasted or overlooked by Him. His insistence on the complete removal of Canaan's idolatry reveals a God who takes seriously anything that might later draw His people's hearts away from exclusive devotion to Him.

crossReferences.note: Uses Israel's wilderness journey as a written example and warning for later believers.
Describes the life of faith as a pilgrimage toward a better, heavenly homeland.
Records the painful consequence of Israel's failure to complete the warning given here about incomplete conquest.
Jesus promises to prepare a place for His people, securing the final destination of their own journey of faith.

study.oneStory: Every stage of Israel's long wilderness journey was significant enough to God to be permanently written down, and their entry into the promised land depended on completing their task fully rather than leaving remnants of the old life behind. This pattern of a recorded, faithful pilgrimage toward a promised inheritance runs through the whole Bible and finds its fullest expression in the Christian life, a journey toward the better, heavenly homeland Christ Himself has gone ahead to prepare.

quiz.q: Who commanded that this journey record be written down, according to verse 2?

options.text: The LORD commanded Moses to record the stages of the journey.
options.note: Verse 2 states this directly.

options.text: Joshua compiled it after Moses' death.
options.note: The text specifies Moses himself recorded it at the LORD's command during his own lifetime.

options.text: The seventy elders wrote it collectively.
options.note: The text attributes the recording specifically to Moses, not the elders.

options.text: No one is credited with recording it.
options.note: Verse 2 credits Moses directly, at the LORD's command.

quiz.q: How did the Israelites leave Egypt, according to verse 3?

options.text: They marched out defiantly in full view of the Egyptians, who were burying their firstborn.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 describe this scene directly.

options.text: They left secretly at night without anyone noticing.
options.note: The text specifically describes them marching out openly, in full view of the Egyptians.

options.text: They were escorted out peacefully by Pharaoh's own army.
options.note: The context, the Egyptians burying their firstborn, shows this followed the plagues, not a peaceful royal escort.

options.text: No description of their departure is given.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 give a vivid description.

quiz.q: Where did Aaron die, according to verses 38 to 39?

options.text: On Mount Hor, at 123 years old, in the fortieth year after leaving Egypt.
options.note: Verses 38 to 39 state this precisely.

options.text: At Kadesh, before Israel ever reached Mount Hor.
options.note: The text specifically places his death on Mount Hor, reached after Kadesh.

options.text: On the plains of Moab, just before entering Canaan.
options.note: That location is where Moses himself would later die, not Aaron, whose death is placed at Mount Hor.

options.text: No location or details are given for his death.
options.note: Verses 38 to 39 give precise details.

quiz.q: What did God command Israel to do to the Canaanite religious sites upon entering the land, according to verse 52?

options.text: Destroy all their carved images and cast idols and demolish all their high places.
options.note: Verse 52 states this command in full detail.

options.text: Preserve them as historical monuments.
options.note: The command is the opposite, complete destruction, not preservation.

options.text: Convert them into Israelite worship sites.
options.note: The command is total demolition, not repurposing the sites.

options.text: No instruction regarding these sites is given.
options.note: Verse 52 gives an explicit and detailed command.

quiz.q: What warning did God give about inhabitants left in the land, according to verses 55 to 56?

options.text: They would become barbs and thorns that harass Israel, and God would do to Israel what He had planned for them.
options.note: Verses 55 to 56 state this warning directly.

options.text: They would eventually convert peacefully to Israel's faith.
options.note: The warning describes ongoing harm and harassment, not eventual peaceful conversion.

options.text: They would pose no lasting threat to Israel's future.
options.note: The text warns explicitly of lasting harm if they remain.

options.text: No warning is given about leaving inhabitants in the land.
options.note: Verses 55 to 56 give a clear and severe warning.

questions: Every single stage of Israel's forty-year journey mattered enough to God to be written down. Where in your own story might you be tempted to dismiss a season as wasted time that God actually values?

The record moves forward camp by camp, never lingering on any single failure or triumph as the whole story. How does seeing your own life as a series of stages, rather than one final verdict, change how you view a current hard season?

God commanded complete removal of anything that could become a snare, rather than partial half-measures. What compromise in your own life do you need to fully remove rather than simply manage or ignore?

The warning about surviving inhabitants becoming barbs and thorns proved tragically true in the book of Judges. What incomplete obedience in your own life risks becoming a lasting source of trouble later?
This journey, however long and winding, was always headed toward a promised inheritance. What promise of God are you currently journeying toward, even through a season that feels like wandering?

translationNotes.note: The note that Israel marched out defiantly, literally with a high hand, an idiom for boldness or defiance, underscores that the exodus was not a fearful flight but a triumphant departure under God's own visible judgment on Egypt's gods.

The place name shifts slightly between Iye-abarim, verse 44, and Iyim, verse 45, most likely an abbreviated form of the same location rather than two separate places, a minor variation common in ancient place-name lists.

Barbs in your eyes and thorns in your sides is vivid Hebrew idiom for a persistent, painful irritant, a warning whose fulfillment is narrated directly in Judges 2:1-3, where an angel of the LORD confirms this very prophecy came true.
